title: Syncopation Introduction — The Anticipated Downbeat
description: Play a simple syncopated pattern where beat 3 is anticipated — the note lands on the 'and of 2' instead of beat 3 itself. This push-pull feel is the foundation of funk, R&B, and soul bass lines.
instructions: Set a metronome to 75 BPM — slower than usual because syncopation is harder to keep steady.
Count '1-and-2-and-3-and-4-and' out loud first, without playing.
The pattern: play on beat 1, skip beat 2, play on the 'and of 2', skip beat 3, play on beat 3, play on beat 4.
The note on the 'and of 2' is the syncopation — it 'anticipates' beat 3 by half a beat.
Keep your foot tapping steady quarter notes the whole time.
If you lose the count, stop and restart from beat 1. Don't try to find your place mid-measure.

variations.label: Harder: anticipate beat 1 of the next measure (the 'and of 4')

tab.label: Anticipated downbeat — crosses the bar line

ensembleNotes: Syncopation is what makes groove music feel like it's leaning forward — like it has momentum and push. The classic James Brown instruction to his band was 'on the one' — meaning lock beat 1 hard, then let the syncopation between beats create the groove. The downbeat is the anchor; the syncopation is the tension between anchors.
